author: @jamest.5001
text: I would like to some day build a large battery, to power my home, charged with solar, and a tiny hydroelectric generator, about 3-4kw, total. And would like around 3k ah at 28v(24-29v) I have a 7 series lithium ion battery now, along with a leadacid Bank, about 450ah, useable capacity,  a small hydrogen fuel cell to charge batteries, or power things would be nice, I also have a 12v LiFePo4 bank 120-130ah for lighting, and small accessories, my lighting is only around 150-200 watts total, 9-18watts per room, I still have mains lighting but it's more efficient to power lights from DC, I have a 10 gauge 12v buss, about 20' from the battery, to power the lights, in a emergency I can charge with my car,  or power direct from my car ,  I would like to build a small steam turbine generator, if I can get atleast 600 watts, anything less, I don't think would be worth the effort,

author: @PressToDigitate
text:
Robert, I just ran across this video after writing you last night.  The residue salts from our Urine-Aluminum/Hydroxide  ("Uralox") Fuel Cell will contain Sodium Aluminate, Aluminum Hydroxide and Aluminum Chloride, and the system can pull Urea out if needed for another process (i.e. your Graphitic Carbon Nitride production).  We plan to recover this residue in swappable Cassettes, for recycling.  If we can incorporate your SWaG technology into the system (together with a Nitinol motor/pump/generator), I feel we can substantially increase the total energy recovered from the 'Canodes' and 'user-supplied BioCatholyte', vs just the DC current, Hydrogen, and 80C process heat as is.

I hope you got my email.  After viewing more of your videos (including the ones on Binder and Separator and Structured Carbon Batteries) and seeing where you're going with all of this, we definitely want to work with RWG on your Group 27 product, as its exactly what I was looking for in my email enquiry to you last night.

author: @alexk6745
text: I also was thinking about that way some time ago. But the problem is that it is very inefficient. Look you need 2Al to get 3H2. So you need 54 grams of AL to get 6 grams of Hydrogen. Moreover you need convert back ALOH3 into AL. Seems very expensive

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Thanks for getting the book mate - I think a better way would be to feed an aluminium wire onto a charged surface at a fixed rate - if the voltage is high the ali will react with the water to give the oxide and hydrogen. The payback is in favour of hydrogen production

author: @JustinHallPlus
text:
Interesting concept.  Does sodium hydroxide react with mineral oil?  I know it reacts with animal fat, but I was thinking that it might be a good idea to have the aluminium in a bath of fluid that wouldn't react with the sodium hydroxide, and then you could control the rate of aluminium consumption by controlling the amount of aluminium that was in contact with the lye solution.

I just bought your Supercapacitors 101 book, thanks for making all this information available.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: fair enough - cheers

author: @mickycheese27
text: What I meant was; as video resolution increases, the number of pixels also increases. However, on some videos ,like this one, the video resolution is high but the pixel count is low, which means the camera's sensors capture more light resulting in higher resolution but due to the low number of sensors the pixel count tends to be low. Different materials from which sensors are built result in different amounts of light being captured. This could be used for night vision and advanced solar panels.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i think electrolysis is a better way to go. That's not to take anything away from your steam idea I just think electrolysis would lead to a simpler more easily maintainable system for anybody to use. It could almost be a plug it in recharging system

author: @joaocarimo
text:
Hi Mr Smith, I know the redox process is really interesting as well, as it is reversive.
I was thinking about using some Heated coal to inject steam and then pass it on the oxide in order to deoxidize back the metal in order to reuse for the next reaction. I was thinking about to replace the car catalyser.. Can you help me here on how to solve the problem of regenerating the metal again? Should I use electrolysis instead?
